teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the idea that the terminal is the everyday control panel for the robotics workflow.
teaching_arc.progression: Introduce the filesystem mental model, then build command confidence through examples and checks.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish more comfortable navigating and reasoning about their Linux workspace.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Give students enough filesystem and terminal fluency that later ROS 2 labs feel manageable instead of overwhelming.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand the basic filesystem layout and navigation commands they will keep using.
Students should be able to move, inspect, and manage files confidently from the terminal.
Students should leave with better debugging habits for path and shell issues.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Avoid sounding like a cheat sheet; explain why each command matters.
Use repetition strategically to build confidence.
transition_style: Move from the mental model to the concrete lab step students will run next, then close each section by explaining what to verify.

slides.narration_seed: Open Linux File System + Terminal Basics by framing the big question of the topic, why it matters in the ROS 2 lab workflow, and what students should be able to do by the end.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Give students enough filesystem and terminal fluency that later ROS 2 labs feel manageable instead of overwhelming.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Three paths you must know so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Linux File System + Terminal Basics as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Linux File System + Terminal Basics connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Linux File System + Terminal Basics sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Use Three paths you must know to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: / = root (top of the whole system)
/home/ = your personal files and projects
~ = shortcut for your home folder
Typical ROS workspace location: ~/ros2_ws
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Linux File System + Terminal Basics into Three paths you must know so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Common folders you will see so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Three paths you must know to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Three paths you must know connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Three paths you must know sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Use Common folders you will see to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: /bin: basic system commands
/etc: configuration files
/usr: most programs and libraries
/opt: optional installs (sometimes large tools)
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Three paths you must know into Common folders you will see so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Terminal habits that save time so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Common folders you will see to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Common folders you will see connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Common folders you will see sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Keep Terminal habits that save time efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Tab = auto-complete (use it constantly)
↑ / ↓ = command history
man = manual (example: man cd) — note: cd is a shell builtin
--help = quick help (example: ls --help)
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep Terminal habits that save time efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on Terminal habits that save time and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Define Navigation (do these now) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: pwd → show current folder
ls → list files
cd → enter a folder
cd .. → go up one folder
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Navigation (do these now) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Navigation (do these now) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Navigation (do these now) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Navigation (do these now) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Navigation (do these now) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Define Files & folders (do these now) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: mkdir lab1 → create a folder
touch hello.txt → create a file
cp hello.txt copy.txt → copy a file
mv copy.txt lab1/ → move into folder
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Files & folders (do these now) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Files & folders (do these now) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Define Viewing text files (useful in ROS) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: cat file.txt → print the whole file
less file.txt → scroll through a file (q to quit)
head file.txt → first lines
tail file.txt → last lines
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Viewing text files (useful in ROS) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Viewing text files (useful in ROS) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Keep Installing software with apt (Ubuntu) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: sudo apt update → refresh package list
sudo apt upgrade -y → upgrade installed packages
sudo apt install → install a package
Example: sudo apt install tree then run tree
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.

slides.explanation_style: Keep Installing software with apt (Ubuntu) efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on Installing software with apt (Ubuntu) and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Define Networking basics (ROS uses networking) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: ping google.com → check internet (Ctrl+C to stop)
ip a → see IP address and interfaces
ssh user@ip → connect to another machine (RPi/robot)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Networking basics (ROS uses networking) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Networking basics (ROS uses networking) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Define Editing files quickly in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: nano file.txt → simple terminal editor (Ctrl+O save, Ctrl+X exit)
gedit file.txt → graphical editor (if desktop available)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Editing files quickly to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Editing files quickly is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Mini practice (do it now) into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: 1) cd ~
2) mkdir ros_practice
3) cd ros_practice
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
4) touch notes.txt
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Mini practice (do it now) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Mini practice (do it now) without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Mini practice (do it now) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Mini practice (do it now) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quick quiz #1 into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: What does ~ mean in Linux?
~ is a shortcut for your home directory.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quick quiz #1 and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quick quiz #2 into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Which command shows your current folder?
pwd prints the current working directory.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quick quiz #2 and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.

slides.narration_seed: Use Quick quiz #3 as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: Which command installs a package on Ubuntu?
sudo apt install installs a package using Ubuntu’s package manager.
Students should understand the basic filesystem layout and navigation commands they will keep using.
Students should be able to move, inspect, and manage files confidently from the terminal.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Quick quiz #3 to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Ready for ROS so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Quick quiz #3 as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Quick quiz #3 connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Quick quiz #3 with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: Define Ready for ROS in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Know: /, /home, ~
Use: cd, ls, pwd, mkdir, touch, cp, mv, rm
Update/install with: sudo apt update, sudo apt install
Next: ROS 2 Humble install + first nodes
